chemistry.reaction_name: Photocatalytic degradation of methyl orange and paracetamol
chemistry.scope_summary: Photocatalytic degradation of methyl orange (30% degradation in 375 min) and paracetamol (65% degradation in 14 h) using TiO2 nanoparticles immobilized on porous silica microspheres under simulated solar illumination in continuous flow reactor

optimization_table.key_findings: TiO2 NPs loaded SiO2 MSs showed best performance with continuous flow reactor under simulated solar light

figures.caption: Figure   2.  Photograph of the experimental set-up (continuous flow) used for the photocatalytic tests. | Figure   1.  Approach for the synthesis of the photocatalytic microspheres.
Figure   2.  Photograph of the experimental set-up (continuous flow) used for the photocatalytic tests. | Figure   1.  Approach for the synthesis of the photocatalytic microspheres.

Figure   3.  Schematic representation of the illuminated sample holder:  1- quartz window to allow exposure to solar light; 2- chamber (holder)  filled with photocatalytic microspheres; 3- filter to ensure that there are  no microspheres released with the flow. The inlet and outlet Teflon tubes  allow the continuous flow of the pollutant through the microspheres. | Figure   4.  a) FTIR-ATR spectra and b) thermogravimetric curves of the TiO 2  NPs dried at 60° C and heat-treated for 1 h at 500° C.

figures.flow_path: two inlet streams merge at T-junction, flow through reactor tube to outlet
figures.key_information: simple T-junction mixer with numbered ports (1, 2, 3) leading to tubular reactor
figures.caption: Figure   3. figures.trend: decreasing for TiO₂ NPs loaded SiO₂ MSs, stable for control samples
figures.caption: Figure   11.  Plots of  C / C o  versus time for the unloaded and TiO 2  NPs loaded  SiO 2  MSs. MO shows no photodegradation at all during the experiment  time, in the absence of photocatalyst. Experimental conditions: 25° C, pH  =  7, mass (TiO2 NPs) /mass (MO)   =  25, 100 mL of 20 ppm MO aq. solution,  300 mg of TiO 2  NPs loaded SiO 2  MSs (66 mg TiO 2 ), flow  =  5 mL min⁻¹ ,  irradian

translation_logic.batch_limitation: Traditional batch photocatalysis requires catalyst separation and recovery after treatment
translation_logic.flow_advantage: Continuous flow allows for easy catalyst recovery using immobilized microspheres with filtration, enabling continuous operation
translation_logic.what_changed: Reactor design changed from batch to continuous flow with immobilized TiO2 nanoparticles on porous silica microspheres
translation_logic.what_stayed_same: Temperature (25°C), light source (solar simulator, 300W), and photocatalyst (TiO2 anatase) remained the same
translation_logic.safety_improvement: Improved catalyst containment and easier separation reduces handling risks

figure_descriptions: Reactor scheme showing illuminated sample holder with quartz window, chamber filled with photocatalytic microspheres, and filter
Optimization plot showing concentration vs time for different catalyst systems

notes: This is a photocatalytic degradation study focused on wastewater treatment rather than synthetic chemistry. The flow system enables continuous operation with easy catalyst recovery. Missing key quantitative data like residence time, exact concentrations, and throughput calculations. Performance metrics given as degradation percentages rather than synthetic yields.
